retrieved_docs.title: Nintedanib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Nintedanib is an indolinone derivative that binds to tyrosine kinase receptors. The mechanism of action is thought to involve the restriction of neoangiogenesis by inhibiting various growth factors such as fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR), vascular endothelial growth factor receptor (VEGFR), platelet-derived growth factor receptor (PDGFR), colony-stimulating factor-1-receptor (CSF1R), and Fms-like tyrosine kinase-3 (FLT3). [13] This drug was initially designed to treat non-small lung cancer. Due to similarities in the growth factors involved in the pathogenesis of this cancer and pulmonary fibrosis, nintedanib was considered a potential treatment for IPF and was studied. [14] Nintedanib competitively binds to the intracellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) binding site of the growth factor receptors, preventing autophosphorylation and blocking downstream signaling cascades. Additionally, nintedanib blocks non-receptor tyrosine kinases (eg, Src, Lck) directly, preventing the activation of fibroblasts. As a result, nintedanib ultimately inhibits fibroblast proliferation and migration, effectively attenuating angiogenesis within the lungs. This reduction in fibrosis and angiogenesis helps to impede the progression of ILD, IPF, and associated complications such as pulmonary hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: Leukemia Cutis -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Initial treatment of myeloid leukemia is based on age, history of prior myelodysplastic syndrome or cytotoxic chemotherapy, and performance status. The use of cytogenetics and molecular markers gained through next-generation sequencing is necessary for risk stratification and can guide upfront frontline therapy. Several molecular markers such as FLT3, NPM1, CEBPA, IDH, And other mutations are important for risk assessment. However, although karyotyping and molecular markers are robust predictors of disease-free survival, induction chemotherapy is often initiated before this information is available. Standard induction regimens have as their basis the backbone of cytarabine in combination with anthracycline popularly known as the 7+3 regimen. The therapy intends to reduce the leukemic burden and restore normal hematopoiesis. For people that are found to be FLT3 mutated, midostaurin, an FLT3 inhibitor to both ITD and TKD mutations, is now added to standard chemotherapy as part of frontline treatment based on the RATIFY Alliance Trial.

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Lenvatinib exerts the mechanism of action via inhibition of multiple receptors of tyrosine kinases: VEGFR-1 (FLT1), VEGFR-2 (KDR), VEGFR-3 (FLT4), FGFR-1, FGFR-2, FGFR-3, FGFR-4, PDGFRa, RET, and c-KIT. Tumor growth depends on developing and proliferating new blood vessels (neovascularization). Tumor growth and angiogenesis occur when the ligands bind to their respective tyrosine kinase receptors in the cellular membrane, initiating an intracellular signal transduction phosphorylation cascade promoting angiogenesis and cell proliferation.

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The inhibition of the VEGF receptors prevents tumor angiogenesis, and the inhibition of FGFR, RET, PDGFRα, and KIT prevents the further proliferation of malignant cells. [5] The concurrent inhibition of both receptor pathways results in the inhibition of nuclear signal transduction and simultaneous suppression of the activity of factors involved in tumor growth.

retrieved_docs.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Angiogenesis is a critical step in cancer growth. VEGF is a key inducer of pathological angiogenesis expressed in nearly all human tumors. [50] Due to VEGF’s role in blood vessel survival and plasticity, TKIs that inhibit VEGFR carry associations with several cardiovascular toxicities, particularly hypertension. [51] [52] This toxicity is likely because VEGF is necessary for adequate nitric oxide production. Inhibition of VEGF, therefore, results in elevated systemic vascular resistance. [53] [54] Because VEGF is vital to endothelial cell survival, anti-VEGF therapy can diminish the integrity and regenerative capacity of endothelial cells, causing pro-coagulant changes. The long-term weakening and diminished integrity of blood vessel walls can eventually lead to thrombosis and hemorrhage. [55] Other adverse events associated with VEGFR TKIs include: renal injury, left ventricular dysfunction, cerebral and intestinal hemorrhage, cardiac ischemia, thrombosis, and skin reactions. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Other clinical uses of targeted TKI therapies include lapatinib as second or third-line treatment in metastatic HER2+ breast cancer, [24] midostaurin in FLT3 mutated acute myeloid leukemia, [19] larotrectinib as salvage therapy in unresectable or metastatic solid tumors containing Trk gene translocations, [33] and imatinib in c-KIT mutated GIST. In c-KIT, mutations in exon 11 convey a better prognosis and response to imatinib than mutations in exon 9. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Emerging and Future Immunotherapy Targets
retrieved_docs.content: Several combinatorial strategies have been proposed to have shown promise in developing future therapeutic strategies against tumors that show resistance to currently available strategies. The Ang/Tie 2 pathway is thought to be responsible for basal angiogenesis and vascular stability following vascular endothelial growth factor blockade. [126] Inhibitors such as trebananib, which block this novel pathway, may have a role in combination with VEGF blockade. [127] ALK 1 inhibitors, such as dalantercept, may have a role in interfering with the formation of the vascular bed. [128] Dual mTOR c1/c2 inhibitors may be a potential therapeutic option since the existing therapies only target them on the TOR c1 pathway. [129] Inhibitors targeting hypoxia-inducible factor 2 and aberrant glycolysis, aberrant glutamine, and tryptophan metabolisms, such as CB 839 (glutamine metabolism) indoleamine 2.3 dioxygenase inhibitors, are being developed with an intent to lead to potential additional benefit. [130] [131] It has been suggested that the relationship between the tumor and it’s immune micro-environment needs to be studied further, with the intent to developing beneficial combinatorial therapy options. Other studied approaches include personalized vaccination, targeted radiotherapy to enhance immune responses, cytoreductive surgery following systemic therapy, and immunotherapy or targeted therapy in the neoadjuvant setting. [132] [133] Several molecules that are being investigated as potential targets include chemokine receptors, lymphocyte activation gene – 3, OX -40 (CD 134), B and T lymphocyte attenuator (BTLA), and V-domain containing immunoglobulin containing suppressor of T – lymphocytes activation, soluble. [134]

retrieved_docs.title: Periocular Capillary Infantile Hemangiomas -- Treatment / Management -- Immunomodulators
retrieved_docs.content: Interferon-alpha (IFN-α) works by downregulating pro-angiogenic factors, eg, FGF and VEGF. [55] IFN-α prevents angiogenesis, or the production of new blood vessels, which is essential to the growth of hemangiomas. IFN-α also boosts the immune system's capacity to control aberrant growth by promoting macrophage and natural killer cell activity. In the past, IFN-α was used to treat hemangiomas that were potentially fatal or functionally impaired, especially when previous treatments had failed. It is applied subcutaneously and takes a long time to work (usually several months). IFN-α can cause hemangiomas to shrink, especially those that are not responsive to corticosteroids or propranolol. Despite its usefulness in certain circumstances, its use has reduced due to concerns about major adverse effects, most notably neurotoxicity leading to spastic diplegia. Safer alternatives have mainly supplanted IFN-α because of the potential of neurodevelopmental adverse effects, such as spasticity and motor delays, which is the main limitation of its usage in babies. Sirolimus is an inhibitor of mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin) with antiangiogenic and immunosuppressive qualities. It obstructs the mTOR pathway, which is connected to angiogenesis, cell division, and growth. [56] By impeding this pathway, sirolimus lessens the aberrant vascular growth observed in hemangiomas. Complex infantile hemangiomas may benefit from sirolimus treatment, especially if they are resistant to propranolol or linked to conditions like PHACE syndrome. Treatment is given orally, and the length of time varies based on how severe the hemangioma is. Sirolimus has had encouraging outcomes in early clinical reports, particularly when treating troublesome hemangiomas because of ulceration, airway involvement, or rapid growth. Notable tumor reduction and symptom relief have been noted in some cases. Although sirolimus is usually well tolerated, it can weaken the immune system, raising the risk of infections. Other adverse effects include hyperlipidemia, gastrointestinal problems, and mouth ulcers.Imiquimod is an immunomodulator applied topically that promotes the synthesis of cytokines important in immunological regulation and antiviral responses, including interleukins, tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), and IFN-α. [57] It might inhibit angiogenesis and encourage the hemangioma tissue to recede by strengthening the local immune response. Imiquimod is used topically and has been investigated for tiny or superficial hemangiomas, especially as a stand-alone treatment or in situations where other therapies are not appropriate. Some studies have suggested imiquimod may help reduce the size and color of superficial hemangiomas. However, the findings have been inconsistent. Usually, imiquimod is only used in minor cases that don't involve significant penetration. Common adverse effects include soreness, redness, and local skin irritation. Because of the topical administration, systemic absorption and extensive immunological modification are less of an issue. Lasers

retrieved_docs.title: Pentosan Polysulfate Maculopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When FGF signaling is inhibited in transgenic zebrafish, the RPE layer thickens significantly as the cells grow to contact the outer segments of the photoreceptors. [22] In humans, certain chemotherapeutic agents target FGF receptors (FGFRs), including erdafitinib (pan-FGFR inhibitor) and pemigatinib (FGFR 1-3), both of which have been shown to induce an increase in reflectivity and thickening of the ellipsoid and interdigitation zones, with subsequent subretinal fluid (SRF) and serous retinal detachment. [23] [24] [25] [26] SRF from chemotherapeutic targeting starts an average of 21 days after starting the medication. Conversely, SRF will often resolve without discontinuation of PPS. Additionally, in one case report, there were no changes to the choroidal vasculature [24] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Aflibercept -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: VEGFR-2 is considered to be the primary mediator of VEGF-induced angiogenesis. Aflibercept inhibits VEGF-A–induced dimerization of VEGFR-2, thereby preventing the activation of intracellular tyrosine kinase domains and reducing downstream signaling pathways, which ultimately minimizes pathological angiogenesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- Ophthalmic
retrieved_docs.content: Central serous retinopathy Retinal pigment epithelial detachment TKI keratitis

retrieved_docs.title: Wet Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD) -- Treatment / Management -- Emerging Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: OPT-302, which targets VEGF-C and VEGF-D KSI-301, which targets VEGF-A Agents that counter platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), including rinucumab, pegpleranib, X-82, DE-120, and CLS-AX Gene therapy, which utilizes various vectors, including adeno-associated virus (AAV), to produce anti-VEGF agents such as VEGF receptor, ranibizumab, aflibercept, endostatin, and angiostatin Anti-tissue factor agents Sustained release devices, including devices for delivering drugs such as ranibizumab and sunitinib (the latter targets VEGF-A and PDGF) Topical medications being evaluated for wet AMD treatment include the following: Agents targeting VEGF-A and PDGF, such as pazopanib, regorafenib, and PAN-90806 Squalamine targets VEGF, PDGF, and basic fibroblast growth factor LHA510 targets tyrosine kinase Oral medications evaluated for managing wet AMD include X-82, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor blocking VEGF and PDGF receptors, and AKST4290, which targets CCR3, the receptor for eotaxin. [90]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Metronomic Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Targeted agents have been used in combination with low-dose cytotoxic chemotherapy to reduce the toxic effects of treatment. Metronomic dosing has been used to enhance the anti-angiogenic effect of targeted therapy by preventing the recruitment of endothelial progenitor cells derived from bone marrow to the tumor vasculature. Though this approach to treatment (a combination of weekly gemcitabine, daily capecitabine with sorafenib) has been associated with partial response in 50 percent of patients, however, the findings have not been replicated. [108]

retrieved_docs.title: Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Keratitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antineoplastic medications are infamous for causing an array of toxicities ranging from dermatological manifestations to cardiovascular side-effects. Physicians face the challenge of having to deal with unexpected complications when administering cancer therapy. The use of targeted cancer treatment, such as tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy, is increasing because it is more effective and less toxic to normal tissue. Tyrosine kinase inhibitor keratitis is an inflammation of the cornea caused by the inhibition of the wound healing and hydrating pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment Strategies Based on Genetic Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: FLT3 mutations in acute myeloid leukemia: Patients with AML who have FLT3-ITD (internal tandem duplication) mutations are at an increased risk of relapse and have a poorer prognosis. Targeted therapies, including FLT3 inhibitors such as midostaurin, quizartinib, and gilteritinib, are integrated into treatment regimens to improve outcomes. These inhibitors are used in combination with standard chemotherapy during the induction and consolidation phases and may continue as maintenance therapy. [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and midostaurin are both classified as protein kinase inhibitors within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system under the category “Other protein kinase inhibitors.” This classification indicates that each drug belongs to the same therapeutic group of agents that target protein kinases, reflecting shared mechanisms of action and relevance in the treatment of diseases such as cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Midostaurin is a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 37
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib; they inhibit the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway and are employed in hematologic malignancies, with dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib and imatinib mesylát indicated for lymphoid leukemia and ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, another protein‑kinase inhibitor subgroup, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib and target Bruton's tyrosine‑kinase; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, highlighting BTK inhibition in lymphoid cancers. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class and exemplify kinase‑targeted therapy beyond leukemias. The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors” also contains agents such as cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin have been identified as potential treatments for acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these protein‑kinase inhibitors—targeting BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases—constitute a network of antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs that underpin contemporary therapeutic strategies for lymphoid and myeloid leukemias and other cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Within this subclass, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are recognized members, and ibrutinib has therapeutic activity against chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Another grouping, designated as other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC, also belongs to the broader class of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin are members of this ATC group, and both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Together, these kinase‑targeting drugs illustrate the role of protein kinase inhibition in the management of hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors—including dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib—are classified as protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; dasatinib, its anhydrous form and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors such as ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to the protein kinase inhibitor, antineoplastic and immunomodulating group, with ibrutinib specifically indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, exemplified by canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, are another subset of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Additional protein kinase inhibitors in this ATC subclass include cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin may be employed in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these kinase‑targeted therapies illustrate how distinct protein kinase inhibitor subclasses are organized within broader antineoplastic categories and matched to specific hematologic and lymphoid malignancies, supporting precise, patient‑centered treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 96
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a comprehensive pharmacologic landscape for blood cancers (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم), particularly leukemias such as lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are classified as protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity; the first three are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, also belong to this class, with ibrutinib specifically used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors used in acute myeloid leukemia include gilteritinib, midostaurin, and cabozantinib. EGFR inhibitors such as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 96
retrieved_docs.content: specifically used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors used in acute myeloid leukemia include gilteritinib, midostaurin, and cabozantinib. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are also noted, reflecting the broader category of kinase‑targeted agents with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Conventional agents employed in blood‑cancer therapy comprise corticosteroids (cortisone acetate, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate), antimetabolites and alkylating agents (cytarabine, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman). Cortisone acetate is highlighted as the active corticosteroid component of an oral liquid botanical formulation used in supportive care, and dexamethasone acetate is also indicated for septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). The summary notes contraindications for patients with blood cancer, specifically

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
retrieved_docs.content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.
